title: Blue Moon
logline: On the opening night of Oklahoma in 1943, lyricist Lorenz Hart spends one final evening at Sardi's confronting his professional dissolution, personal demons, and complicated relationship with his former partner Richard Rodgers.
antagonistOrConflict: Hart's alcoholism, professional obsolescence, and the painful reality of being replaced by Oscar Hammerstein II as Rodgers' collaborator

actBreaks.description: Hart's confrontation with Rodgers at their private table establishes the central conflict of their dissolved partnership and Hart's decline.

validation.ruling: Rodgers offers Hart a chance to collaborate on reviving A Connecticut Yankee, representing a major turning point that shifts the story from Hart's passive suffering to active hope for redemption.

actBreaks.description: Hart returns to the main bar after his devastating private conversation with Rodgers, marking his final acceptance of his isolation.

validation.ruling: Elizabeth returns with her young suitor George Roy Hill, fundamentally changing Hart's situation from private longing to public confrontation with her romantic life and his own irrelevance.

naturalStructure.structuralPersonality: This is a chamber piece masquerading as a biographical drama—essentially a real-time psychological autopsy of a dying artist. The script uses the confined space of Sardi's restaurant as a pressure cooker, trapping Hart in a series of increasingly painful encounters that strip away his defenses. The structure mirrors the evening's progression from celebration to devastation, with Hart moving through different areas of the restaurant like stations of the cross. Each location change represents a deeper level of his psychological descent, from the public bar area where he can still perform his wit, to the private dining room where his vulnerabilities are exposed, to the intimate coat room where he makes his final desperate connection with Elizabeth.
naturalStructure.pacingNotes: The pacing is deliberately theatrical, with long dialogue scenes that build tension through conversation rather than action. The extended sequence in the dining room (scene 14) serves as the emotional centerpiece, while the coat room scenes (22-23) provide the most intimate and revealing moments. The script maintains momentum through Hart's manic energy and sharp dialogue, even as the dramatic stakes intensify.

incitingIncident.description: Elizabeth arrives at the bar, Hart greets her enthusiastically, compliments her new blonde hair, and gives her the flowers he ordered.
incitingIncident.sceneHeading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS

validation.ruling: Elizabeth's arrival at the party and revelation that she lost her virginity to Cooper shatters Hart's ordinary world and establishes the central emotional conflict of his unrequited love.

midpoint.description: Hart returns to the bar, drinks whiskey, and tells White he feels he's entered the 'for worse' part of his life, feeling superannuated and abandoned by God.
midpoint.sceneHeading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS

validation.ruling: Scene 14 is already identified as an act break. Scene 15 serves as the midpoint where Hart returns to the bar, drinks whiskey, and tells White he feels he's entered the 'for worse' part of his life, marking his shift from hope to despair and acceptance of his obsolescence.

climax.description: Rodgers enters the coat room to get his coat, Hart introduces Elizabeth as his protégé, and she lavishly praises Oklahoma to Rodgers before they leave together.
climax.sceneHeading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - COAT ROOM AREA - CONTINUOUS

validation.ruling: Hart's introduction of Elizabeth to Rodgers as his protégé reaches peak dramatic tension as she lavishly praises Oklahoma and accepts Rodgers' offer to read her work, completing Hart's emotional arc of loss and replacement.

scenes.heading: EXT. NEW YORK ALLEYWAY - NIGHT
scenes.summary: Lorenz Hart stands alone in a rainy New York alley, talk-singing fragments of his own song while lurching among garbage cans.

scenes.heading: INT. ST. JAMES THEATER - NIGHT
scenes.summary: Hart sits with his mother Frieda in a theater box watching Oklahoma, becomes miserable during the cowboy chorus, and leaves to get a drink.

scenes.heading: EXT. 44TH STREET - NEW YORK CITY - NIGHT
scenes.summary: Hart crosses the street toward a bar/restaurant.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - ENTRACE/MAIN BAR AREA - NIGHT
scenes.summary: Hart enters Sardi's bar where bartender Eddie playfully aims a pistol at him, demanding he put down a shopping bag, while pianist Morty Rifkin plays in military uniform.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: A flower delivery man brings Hart modest carnations for five dollars, Hart tips him and shares that Elizabeth mailed him a pencil from New Haven.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Hart toasts the past, discusses Oklahoma with Eddie and Morty, criticizing the show's exclamation point and expressing his disdain for its optimistic themes.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: The delivery man returns with expensive flowers for Richard Rodgers, Hart reads the card quoting Oscar Hammerstein's lyrics and reacts with dismay.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Elizabeth arrives at the bar, Hart greets her enthusiastically, compliments her new blonde hair, and gives her the flowers he ordered.
scenes.turningPointNote: Elizabeth's arrival sets the romantic subplot in motion and establishes Hart's infatuation

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: After Elizabeth leaves, Hart tells Eddie he's writing a musical about her called 'Why We Fuck' and contemplates drinking whiskey to get through congratulating Rodgers.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S - WHITE’S TABLE - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Hart approaches writer E.B. White's table, recognizes him, and begins telling him about Elizabeth, explaining how she never asks about him but he finds her genuinely more interesting.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S - MORTY’S STATION - MAIN BAR AREA -CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: The men sing 'Over There' while Hart makes dark jokes about war casualties, discussing with White the massive death toll and questioning God's perspective on human warfare.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Hart tells White about inviting Elizabeth to spend a weekend with him in Vermont, describing his excitement when she agreed without hesitation.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA / LOBBY - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Hart goes to check his appearance in the mirror while Eddie and White discuss whether Hart slept with Elizabeth, speculating about her motivations.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - SMALL TABLE - DINING ROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Rodgers pulls Hart to a private table to discuss reviving 'A Connecticut Yankee' with new songs, but Hart struggles to focus as party guests interrupt with congratulations.
scenes.turningPointNote: Rodgers offers Hart a chance to collaborate again, representing a potential lifeline for Hart's career

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Hart returns to the bar, drinks whiskey, and tells White he feels he's entered the 'for worse' part of his life, feeling superannuated and abandoned by God.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MEN’S ROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: In the men's room, Hart tells Morty about crying over a harsh review of 'Pal Joey' and being comforted by Vivienne Segal.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN DINING AREA/LOBBY - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Rodgers stands with Oscar Hammerstein and friends, reading aloud positive reviews of Oklahoma while the crowd celebrates.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - STAIRCASE LANDING - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Hart and Rodgers have an emotional moment on the staircase where Hart reminisces about discovering Rodgers' talent, awkwardly hugs him, and Rodgers reassures him about future collaborations.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S - MAIN BAR AREA/WHITE’S TABLE - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Hart returns to find White has left, drinks more whiskey, and jokes to Morty about writing 'the ineffectual blues' while calling himself unemployable.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Elizabeth returns with her young suitor George Roy Hill, who asks for Hart's autograph while Elizabeth mentions Oscar Hammerstein called her 'a gem.'

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - ENTRANCE AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Oscar Hammerstein approaches Hart and Elizabeth, acknowledging the difficult night for Hart and asking if he genuinely liked the show.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - COAT ROOM AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Hart and Elizabeth retreat to the coat room where she tells him about losing her virginity to someone named Cooper, describing the encounter in intimate detail.

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - COAT ROOM AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Rodgers enters the coat room to get his coat, Hart introduces Elizabeth as his protégé, and she lavishly praises Oklahoma to Rodgers before they leave together.
scenes.turningPointNote: Elizabeth leaves with Rodgers, devastating Hart and symbolizing his complete replacement both professionally and personally

scenes.heading: INT. SARDI’S BAR - MAIN BAR AREA/LOBBY - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Alone as the party ends, Hart drops his bag of presents and cards, slowly picking them up while talking about his arthritis and plans to bring back wearing cloaks.
